text: The video shows the uterus plastered to the anterior abdominal wall, appearing grossly adenomyotic, with the sigmoid colon also adhered to the anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon uses a three-port laparoscopic technique, including a suprapubic port, to perform adhesiolysis. Bipolar shearer and Bissinger bipolar instruments are used to dissect and mobilize the bowel, retracting it from the adhesion plane. The parasygmoidal fat is liberated from the anterior abdominal wall, revealing new tissue planes. The dissection exposes the uterus, fundus, and anterior wall, with visible adhesions between the bowel and bladder.

The surgeon gently separates the fatty tissue from the adhesions of the bowel, revealing a virgin plane anterior to the bladder. The round ligament is identified as a landmark, aiding in distinguishing the bladder from the bowel. The surgeon continues to liberate endometriotic and adenomyotic adhesions from the uterus, carefully navigating around surface nodules that attract adhesions.

The surgeon performs gentle adhesiolysis, lifting the peritoneum of the uterine cycle to identify and separate the sigmoid colon from the fat plane. A fine plane of adhesion is visible, and care is taken to avoid tearing it to prevent bleeding. An endometriotic nodule adherent between the bowel and the posterior serosal aspect of the uterus is coagulated and pushed down, liberating bowel adhesions. The bowel is mobilized, revealing a healthy-looking ovary. The round ligament is distorted, forming a V shape, indicating severe adhesions. The bowel is successfully separated from the bladder.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the bladder, starting with the uterovesical fold of peritoneum, which is plastered to the uterus and anterior abdominal wall. The peritoneum is lifted, and dissection proceeds from lateral to medial or centrally, depending on visibility. Downward traction is applied to tent the peritoneum, allowing entry into the space between the bladder and uterus or cervix, facilitating the separation of the bladder.

The surgeon has opened the plane below the bladder, separating it from the cervico-vaginal margins, revealing the vagina and cervix. The bladder is adherent to the uterus along a single line of adhesion, which also connects the uterus to the anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon gently separates the adhesions, mobilizing the uterus within the pelvis. Electrocoagulation is used to liberate the uterus from the anterior abdominal wall, while the bladder remains adherent. The uterine serosa is intact, indicating precise dissection. The bladder is then fully separated, completing the adhesiolysis. The uterus is now mobile, and the surgeon prepares to begin the hysterectomy.

The surgeon separates the cervico-vaginal fascia and cuts the uterosacral ligaments, identifying and opening the vaginal vault. The specimen is retrieved through morcellation, and the vault is closed with continuous polyglactin sutures. Minimal bleeding is observed in the pouch of Douglas, approximately five to ten milliliters, following the adhesiolysis.
